model_narrative: We fit a censored least-absolute-deviations regression to a sample of households. We choose a regression coefficient for each explanatory variable, and for each household we track the fitted censored prediction together with the positive and negative pieces of its residual against the observed response. The objective is to make the total absolute deviation as small as possible, minimizing the sum over all households of both the positive and the negative residual pieces.

sets.name: T
sets.doc: the sample households (observations) in the survey, one index per record

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the explanatory variables (regressors) of the linear predictor, including an intercept term

params.name: Xnms
params.doc: the normalized design matrix giving the value of each regressor for each household; columns are mean and variance normalized

params.name: ynms
params.doc: the normalized left-censored dependent variable (observed response) for each household

params.name: omega
params.doc: a tight valid big-M coefficient per household, used to switch the two sides of the max disjunction on or off; nonnegative

params.name: RHS
params.doc: the normalized censoring threshold below which the response is censored; the floor value of the max

params.name: delta
params.doc: the box domain half-width allowed for each estimated coefficient; nonnegative

vars.name: beta
vars.doc: the estimated regression coefficient for each explanatory variable; unrestricted in sign

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: for each household the value of the linear predictor passed through a max against the censoring threshold, that is the larger of the linear predictor and the threshold

vars.name: gamma
vars.doc: a per-household binary selector that picks which branch of the max disjunction is active for that household

vars.name: sm
vars.doc: the negative-side slack capturing the part of the residual where the prediction exceeds the response

vars.name: sp
vars.doc: the positive-side surplus capturing the part of the residual where the response exceeds the prediction

constraints.name: phi[t]
constraints.intent: For each household the censored prediction must be at least as large as that household's linear predictor, which is the sum across all explanatory variables of the regression coefficient times the household's value of that variable.

constraints.name: phi[t]
constraints.intent: For each household the censored prediction must be at least the censoring threshold, so the prediction never falls below the floor set by that threshold.

constraints.name: phi[t]
constraints.intent: For each household the censored prediction must not exceed its linear predictor unless the branch selector for that household turns this side off. When the selector is set so this branch is active, the prediction is capped at the linear predictor; when the selector is set the other way, the big-M allowance for that household relaxes the cap so it does not bind.

constraints.name: phi[t]
constraints.intent: For each household the censored prediction must not exceed the censoring threshold unless the branch selector for that household turns this side off. When the selector is set so this branch is active, the prediction is capped at the threshold; when the selector is set the other way, the big-M allowance for that household relaxes the cap so it does not bind.

constraints.name: ynms[t]
constraints.intent: For each household the observed response must equal the censored prediction adjusted by the two residual pieces, so the response minus the prediction plus the negative-side slack minus the positive-side surplus comes out to zero.

query: The four estimated coefficients currently net out to roughly minus seventeen. Our methods group is floating a sign-balance requirement: summed across the marriage rating, age, years married, and the intercept, the coefficients have to come out non-negative as a package. What total absolute deviation does the refit report under that requirement?
expected_pyomo: model.c_beta_net_nonneg = Constraint(expr=sum(model.beta[pp] for pp in model.p) >= 0)

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep the estimator from writing off any single record: for every household in the sample, the negative-side and positive-side residual pieces added together shouldn't exceed 3.0. If we refit with that per-household cap in force, where does the total absolute deviation land?

query: Right now only a token handful of households get fitted above the censoring threshold. We're looking at a coverage scenario where the per-household branch selectors have to place at least 25 households on the uncensored side of the disjunction. What does the optimizer return for the deviation total in that case?
expected_pyomo: model.c_uncensored_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.gamma[t] for t in model.T) >= 25)

query: Two reviewer requests arrived bundled as a single revision package: first, the age and intercept coefficients together shouldn't drag the predictor down by more than twenty — their sum has to stay at minus twenty or above; second, the years-married effect has to hold at twelve or higher. Each clamp looks harmless on its own, but plug both in at once and walk me through the deviation total the refit comes back with.

query: Nearly all of the deviation mass sits on one side of this fit — the observed response running above the prediction — which reads as systematic under-prediction to me. Shouldn't the surplus-side residual, totaled over the whole sample, be held to at most ninety percent of the sum of the slack-side residuals plus the surplus-side residuals across all households? What's the estimator seeing that makes such a lopsided fit optimal?
expected_pyomo: model.why_sp_share = Constraint(expr=sum(model.sp[t] for t in model.T) <= 0.9 * sum(model.sm[t] + model.sp[t] for t in model.T))

query: The years-married coefficient lands at about +12.6 while age sits near -9.6, so years married outweighs the age pull outright — that ordering looks backwards for this survey. Shouldn't the years-married effect stay no larger than the magnitude of the negative age effect, that is, at most minus one times the age coefficient? What's the tradeoff I'm missing?

query: Households 1, 2, and 3 all get parked on the censored branch by the estimator, even though they're the very first cases the survey team double-checked by hand. Shouldn't at least two of those three branch selectors be switched on — treated as genuinely uncensored observations? Walk me through what's pulling the model the other way.

query: Every regressor in this design matrix was normalized to unit variance, yet the fitted coefficients run as deep as minus eighteen — on standardized data I'd expect nothing outside single digits. Shouldn't each coefficient, intercept included, be confined to the band between minus eight and plus eight? What's the model seeing that justifies coefficients that large?
